translations.translation:
Liu Fang (Ziqi)
劉放 (子棄)
Lived ADca.170–250

byZL181
onAugust 31st, 2025

Liu Fang, styledZiqi, was fromZhuo commandery[涿郡].
He was a descendant of the son of [Liu] Hong [劉宏], theTownship Marquisof Xi [西鄉侯], who was the son of theDeferentialPrinceofGuangyang[廣陽順王] underHan.1•••
ThePrinceofGuangyanghere is Liu Jian [劉建], grandson ofEmperor Liu Chethrough his son Liu Dan [劉旦]. His posthumous title is recorded in theBook of Hanas the “Courteous[頃]”, not the “Deferential[順]”. TheBook of Hanrecords the name of Liu Hong [劉宏] as Liu Rong [劉容] instead.↩
He successively served his commandery in its government, and was nominated asFilial and Incorrupt. It happened that the world fell into great chaos. At the time,Wang SongofYuyang[漁陽] occupied his land.[Liu] Fangwent to serve him. When theGrand ProgenitorovercameJi province[冀州],[Liu] Fangtold[Wang] Song,
“In the past, whenDong Zhuoacted treacherously, the eminent and mighty all rose up, but they relied on their troops and acted arbitrarily; these people grew themselves [in power]. OnlyExcellency Caois capable of rescuing all from the turmoil. He assisted and supported theSon of Heaven, accepting imperial orders to subjugate the guilty. Wherever he went, he always triumphed. As for the two brutish Yuans, they suffered the great loss atHuainan[淮南] when they defended and they suffered the crushing defeat atGuandu[官渡] when they fought. Riding on those victories, he rolled them up like a mat and he will soon bring peace to theHeshuo Region[河朔]. With stern punishments, he brought unity. The overall situation can be seen. Those who first joined were shown fortune while those that later submitted first suffered ruin. This is the time not to wait until the end of the day, but to quickly take action! Of old, whenQing Buabandoned his imperial ambitions to submit toHanwith a wielded sword, he really understood the truth between destruction and ascension and knew the distinction between leaving and joining.General, you should tender yourself and devote your service. You would be generously received!”
[Wang] Songconcurred with him. It happened that when theGrand ProgenitorsuppressedYuan TanatNanpi[南皮], he had a letter recruit[Wang] Song.[Wang] SongraisedYongnu[雍奴],Quanzhou[泉州], andAnci[安次] to submit to him.[Liu] Fangwrote a reply message to theGrand Progenitorfor[Wang] Song, with the writing most elegant. TheGrand Progenitorappreciated this, and as he also heard about the persuasion [ofWang Song], recruited[Liu] Fang.
In the tenth year of theera [205], he and[Wang] Songboth arrived. TheGrand Progenitorwas very delighted and told[Liu] Fang, “Of old, whenBan BiaoservedDou Rong, he earned merits in theHexi region[河西]!2How similar is our present situation!” Thus,[Liu] Fangwas madeMilitary Adjutantunder theExcellency of Works, then successively served asMaster of RecordsandRecorder, then left to serve asPrefectofHeyang[郃陽令],Duiyu[祋祤令], andZan[贊令].A•••
Ban Biao[班彪], a subordinate of warlordDou Rong[竇融], was crucial in taking that area for theHanthrough advisingDou Rongto surrender to the dynasty.↩
Dui [祋] has the fanqie of Du [都] and Huo [活]. Yu [祤] is pronounced Xu [詡].
When theState of Weiwas established, he andSun ZiofTaiyuan[太原] both served asGentlemen of Confidential Depository. Previously,[Sun] Zialso successively served as prefect of counties, and served asMilitary Adjutantunder theExcellency of Works.BAfter theCivil Emperorascended the throne,[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zishifted to become Left and RightDeputies. After several months,[Liu] Fangwas moved to become theDirector. In the beginning of the, the Confidential Depository was changed to the Central Depository.[Liu] Fangbecame itsSupervisorwhile[Sun] Zibecame itsDirector. They each becamePalace Servant.[Liu] Fangwas gifted peerage ofMarquis within the Passeswhile[Sun] ZibecameMarquis in the Passes, thus controlling privy information. In the third year [222],[Liu] Fangwas promoted in peerage toDistrict Marquisof Weishou [魏壽亭侯] while[Sun] ZibecameMarquis within the Passes. After theBrightEmperorascended the throne, they especially received favor and responsibility. They both were given the title ofCavalier Regular Attendant.[Liu] Fangwas promoted in peerage toTownship Marquisof Xi [西鄉侯] while[Sun] ZibecameDistrict MarquisofLeyang[樂陽亭侯].C
TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zistates:[Sun] Ziwas styledYanlong. He was intelligent and keen at a young age. At three years of age, he lost both of his parents. He grew up under his older brother and sister-in-law. He studied at theGrand University[太學] and perused written records.Wang Yunof the same commandery was awed by him in only their first meeting. When theGrand ProgenitorbecameExcellency of Works,[Sun] Ziwas recruited. However, it happened that his older brother was murdered by a fellow villager. As[Sun] Ziwielded a blade to avenge the feud, he led his family members to escape and reside inHedong[河東], thus he did not respond to the order. Soon, he was given an appointment by his home commandery, but he declined out of illness. His friend,Jia KuiofHedong[河東], told[Sun] Zi, “You are befitting an extraordinary talent. It has happened that your former state is falling into decline. Its master and generals are eagerly attentive and the people living throughout thousands of miles are craning their necks for you. You should be honoring how the ancients value the righteousness of mulberry and catalpa trees.3Yet, you have instead been dawdling for so long, rejecting commands from your lord. This is like shining theJade of Mister Hein the court of theKingofQin, but then concealing it with the value of linked cities.4I personally think you should not adopt that, sir!”[Sun] Ziwas touched by his words, so he went to respond. When he arrived, he was made provisionalOfficer of Meritand nominated asAccounting Official. When theDirector of the Secretariat,Xun Yu, met[Sun] Zi, he sighed and said, “The northern provinces have inherited destruction for so long, I thought the virtuous and wise within them scattered away. But today, I have seenLord Sun!” He petitioned for him to stay and serve asGentleman of the Secretariat. He refused, claiming his family was in trouble, and returned toHedong[河東].•••
Here, “mulberry and catalpa trees” [桑梓] is a metaphor for a hometown because people in ancient times often planted them next to their homes.Jia Kuiis using it to encourageSun Zito go back to his home commandery and take a position there.↩
TheJade of Mister Hewas renowned for its beauty and auspiciousness, said to be extremely coveted and priceless. TheKingof theQinheld hegemony around this time and asked the state of Zhao for theJade of Mister Hein exchange the value of linked cities. The state of Zhao had to send an envoy with theJade of Mister Heto the court of theQin, and the envoy showed theJade of Mister Heto him. The envoy later realized that theKingwas never going to trade cities for the jade, and so snuck away with the jade.Jia Kuiseems to be saying thatSun Ziis underestimating his extraordinary talent like devaluing theJade of Mister Heto the price of linked cities.↩
TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zistates: WhenZhuge Liangmarched toNanzheng[南鄭], debaters believed that a large dispatch of troops ought to be sent to immediately suppress him. TheEmperoralso agreed with this, but he consulted[Sun] Zi.[Sun] Zistated, “When theMartialAugust Emperorwent to conquerNanzheng[南鄭] to seizeZhang Lu, it was at the battle ofYangping[陽平之役] that he was in peril, only being aided later on. He also personally went to retrieve the army ofXiahou Yuan, saying on numerous occasions, 'Nanzheng[南鄭] is a natural prison, for withinXiegu Route[斜谷道], there is five hundredliof stone caves.’ Such words spoke of its great danger and his joy at retrieving the army of[Xiahou] Yuan. TheMartialAugust Emperorwas a sage at using troops. Observing the treacherousShucrows who were on mountains and cliffs, and watching the vileWuvermin who were on rivers and lakes, he would hinder and evade them both. He did not demand for the efforts of the generals and soldiers, nor did he fight due to a moment of fury. He can truly be said to be someone who fights when he sees victory and retreats when he knows jeopardy. Now we are to advance the army toNanzheng[南鄭] in order to suppress[Zhuge] Liang. The roads there are treacherous and restrictive. I have calculated that if we are to deploy elite troops and transfer the garrisons in four provinces within the southern regions5to block and defend the naval rebels, we would be deploying one hundred and fifty thousand to one hundred and sixty thousand soldiers, and we would certainly have to dispatch men over and over again. The realm is in a restless state of the realm and we would be spending a tremendous amount of strength. This is truly worth the deep consideration ofYour Majesty. The manpower for a defensive war requires three times the amount of forced labor. However, seeing the military now, you should give separate orders to the major generals and have them occupy critical points. This show of force will be enough to terrorize our strong foes and quell the border regions. Our generals and soldiers would be as sleeping tigers and the common people would be without disturbance. In the span of several years, the central empire will prosper each day while the two caitiffs ofWuandShuwill destroy themselves.” TheEmperorthus stopped. At the time, a person ofWu,Peng Qi, began an uprising in theJiangnan region[江南], and so debaters thought to use this as an opportunity to conquer it, being sure of victories. TheEmperorconsulted[Sun] Zi, and[Sun] Zisaid, “The clans and people ofPoyang[鄱陽] have begun uprisings on numerous occasions in their history. Their forces are weak and their strategies are shallow, quickly separating and dispersing. In the past, theCivil August Emperoronce secretly asked me about the situation of thetraitors, saying, ‘AtDongpu[洞浦], I killed ten thousand soldiers and seized ten million ships. Within a few days, my sailors shall assemble again.’Jiangling[江陵] was besieged for several months, yet[Sun] Quanselected several hundreds soldiers out of a thousand to station at the eastern gates, and his lands did not fall. He had rules and restrictions in place that both superior and inferior respectfully upheld with manifest results. With this, we can infer that[Peng] Qi—I fear—will be unable to be a major ache close to[Sun] Quan.”[Peng] Qiwas indeed soon defeatedafterwards.•••
The four provinces areJing province,Xu province,Yang province,Yu province.↩
During the end of theera,Wusent generalZhou Heto sail across the ocean toLiaodong[遼東] to beckonGongsun Yuan. TheEmperorwished to block and suppress him. Among those who discussed in the court, many believed it should not be done. Only[Sun] Ziwas determined to enact the plan. Indeed, they were crushed. For this, he was advanced in peerage to becomeTownship Marquisof Zuo [左鄉侯].D
TheChronicles of the Wei Domainstates: TheColonel of the Wuhuan,Tian Yu, commandedXieguiniand others of the western Xianbei to leave their camp to suppressKe BinengandZhiyuzhujian, defeating them. When they returned to the old city ofMayi[馬邑],[Ke] Binengled thirty thousand cavalry to besiege[Tian] Yu. When theEmperorheard this, he did not yet have a plan, so he went to the Central Depository to ask itsSupervisorand itsDirector.DirectorSun Zireplied, “TheGrand AdministratorofShanggu,Yan Zhi[上谷太守], is the younger brother of[Yan] Rou. He has long been trusted by[Ke] Bineng. You should quickly dispatch an edict to him and order him to persuade[Ke] Bineng. We can resolve this without toiling an army.” TheEmperorheeded him.[Ke] Binengindeed released of[Tian] Yuand returned.
[Liu] Fangwas good at making letters and denunciations. Of the edicts and orders of the Three Progenitors6beckoning enemies for them to receive amnesty, the majority were written by[Liu] Fang. In the beginning of theera,Sun QuanandZhuge Liangallied and intended to march out to invade together. When the border lookouts obtained a letter by[Sun] Quan,[Liu] Fangchanged its diction, swapping the original text in many parts and then embellishing it. He made it address theGeneral who Conquers the East,Man Chong, with the intention of returning to the proper moral influence. He then sealed it and showed it to[Zhuge] Liang.[Zhuge] Liangrode toWumajor general,Bu Zhi, among others, so as to meet[Sun] Quan.[Sun] Quanfeared that[Zhuge] Liangsuspected him, so he explained himselfprofoundly.•••
Referring toCao Cao,Cao Pi, andCao Rui.↩
That year [234], they both were given the titles ofPalace AttendantandBrilliantly Blessed Grandee.EIn the second year of theera [238],Liaodong[遼東] was pacified. As they had achievements in strategizing, they were each advanced in peerage, being enfeoffed to their home counties.[Liu] FangbecameMarquisofFangcheng[方城侯] while[Sun] ZibecameMarquisofZhongdu[中都侯].
TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zistates: At the time,Sun QuanandZhuge Liangwere known as mighty foes and there was no year that did not have army expeditions. When theEmperorpresided over the ministers, the making of plans to repel enemies and the creation of strategies to triumph were all handled by[Sun] Zi. Though he received heavy trust, he would often delegate matters to theEmperor, saying, “In mustering large armies and undertaking major decisions, you should do them together with the other ministers. This will display your intelligence, but also broaden your considerations.” Whenever the court ministers had discussions,[Sun] Ziwould memorialize about what was right and what was wrong, choosing the good ones and advancing and accomplishing them. Yet, he would never flaunt his own virtue. If the masses spoke of condemning faults as well as what they loved and what they hated, he would always intercede and resolve, stopping the beginning of insidious slander. In the case of theGeneral who Conquers the East,Man Chong, and theInspectorofLiang province[涼州刺史],Xu Miao, they both were slandered and defamed.[Sun] Ziwould promote and explain their pure conduct, causing them to be without any minute faults in the end. That[Man] Chongand[Xu] Miaowere able to retain their accomplishments and reputation was due to the effort of[Sun] Zi. Previously, when[Sun] Ziwas in his home state, his reputation was more prominent than those of similar ranking. His townspeople, among them abureaucratunder theExcellency of Works,Tian Yu, and theChancellorofLiang[梁相],Zong Yan, were all deeply envious of him.Yang Fengassociated himself with[Tian] Yuand the others and especially formulated defamations against[Sun] Zi. Their hatred was this deep.[Sun] Zidid not make any comments and ultimately did not have any resentment.[Tian] Yu, among others, felt ashamed and confessed, begging to resolve their past ill will and to join together through marriage.[Sun] Zitold him, “I have no ill feelings and I do not know what is there to resolve. This is was you belittling me, then favoring me, sir!” Thus, his oldest son,[Sun] Hong, took his daughter. When he was in prestigious positions,Tian Yufell ill due to old age and was in his household.[Sun] Zitreated him with great generosity, sent his son to his home commandery, and nominated him asFilial and Incorrupt. Later, the son ofYang Fengserved as an official of the Imperial Manufactory. TheEmperorcondemned him for his official duties and intended to deliver him to the law, but[Sun] Ziasked for clemency. He did not remember former ill deeds as in these instances.
That year, theEmperorfell ill and bedridden. He wanted to have thePrinceofYan[燕王],[Cao] Yu, serve asGrand General, and have him, theGeneral of the Leading Army,Xiahou Xian; theGeneral of the Martial Guards,Cao Shuang; theColonel of the Garrison Cavalry,Cao Zhao; and theGeneral of Coursers,Qin Lang, jointly assist in governance.[Cao] Yuwas respectful and decent by nature, tendering a firm resignation with a sincere explanation. TheEmperorsought to meet[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zi, and when they entered the bedchambers, he asked, “Why is thePrinceofYan[燕王] acting thusly?”[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zireplied, “ThePrinceofYan[燕王] truly knows that he cannot handle such a great obligation.” TheEmperorsaid, “CanCao Shuangreplace[Cao] Yuor not?”[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Ziendorsed and achieved it. They also made deep explanations that theGrand Commandant, theCommanding Kingof the Sima clan, should be quickly summoned to maintain the imperial household. TheEmperoraccepted their words. He gave yellow paper to[Liu] Fangto make the edict. When[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zileft, theEmperorchanged his mind and issued an edict halting theCommanding Kingand forbade him from coming. Soon, he met[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Ziagain and told them, “I summoned theGrand Commandant, butCao Zhaoand the others instead had me halt him. They almost ruined my work!” They were ordered to make another edict. TheEmperorsummoned[Cao] Shuangas well as[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zionly. They all received the imperial edict. Thus,[Cao] Yu,[Xiahou] Xian,[Cao] Zhao, and[Qin] Langwere removed from their offices. When theGrand Commandantalso came, he ascended the bed to receive the edict. Afterwards, theEmperorpassedaway.F
TheSayings of the Worldstates:[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Ziwere in charge of crucial responsibilities for a long time.[Xiahou] Xianand[Cao] Zhaofelt uneasy within their hearts. Within the palace, there was a tree fowls would perch upon. The two men said to each other, “They, too, have been there for a long time. How much longer will they be there?” They were referring to[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zi.[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Ziwere fearful and urged theEmperorto summon theCommanding King. TheEmperorhandwrote an edict and ordered theAttendant,Bixie, to go give it to theCommanding King. TheCommanding Kingwas inJi[汲], and[Xiahou] Xianand the others had sent an edict to him beforehand to make him return westward toChang’an[長安] byZhi Pass[軹關]. WhenBixiearrived, theCommanding Kingsuspected there were developments, so he summoned and askedBixieabout the situation. He then rode aDashing Chariotto go back to thecapital. TheEmperorasked[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zi, “Who can be the partner for theGrand Commandant?”[Liu] Fangsaid, “Cao Shuang.” TheEmperorsaid, “Can he handle such a task or not?”[Cao] Shuangwas by his side, crying and unable to respond.[Liu] Fangstepped on his leg and [whispered] in his ear, “I, your minister, will serve theAltars of State, even with death.” The younger brother ofCao Zhao,[Cao] Zuan, wasMajorunder theGrand General. ThePrinceofYan[燕王] had lost favor. When[Cao] Zhaoleft,[Cao] Zuanmet him and said in a startled tone, “TheSovereignis uncertain. How can we all leave together? We should return back!” It was already dusk and[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Ziproclaimed the edict from the palace gates, forbidding[Cao] Zhaoand the others from going in again and removing thePrinceofYan[燕王]. The next day,[Cao] Zhaoarrived at the gates, but he could not go in. He was terrified. He went to theMinister of Justiceand because he mishandled matters, he was removed. TheEmperortold[Xiahou] Xian, “I have already decided on my selections. Leave.”[Xiahou] Xianshed tears and left. He, too, was removed.
What theSayings of the Worldestablished before and after does not match the base biography.
TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zistates: TheEmperortold[Sun] Zi, “I am gradually getting older. I have read many books and commentaries, all praiseworthy; there is not one that I have not read. In my plans for matters ten thousand years in the future, there is nothing more crucial than tasking someone intimate to have wide control over government power and also possessing heavy military responsibilities. The title ofColonel who Shoots at Soundis vacant as of now, and I have long desired to obtain someone intimate. Who can I appoint?”[Sun] Zisaid, “Your Majestyhas profound thinking and prescient ruminations. Truly, this foolish minister of yours cannot ever match you. Everything within the records and biographies have all been studied in your sagacious purview. In the past, had theSupreme[Progenitor] ofHanbeen unaware that[Chen] Pingand [Zhou] Bo [周勃] would be able to safeguard theLiu clan, had theFilialandMartial[Emperor]been unaware that Jin [Midi] [金日磾] andHuo [Guang]could be entrusted with [future] affairs, then the danger cannot even be described! During the time theCivilAugust Emperorfirst summonedCao Zhento return, he personally ordered me to make thorough considerations. Upon his departure,Your Majestyascended the throne steps. You still had Cao Xiu, who had prestige externally and internally, and you were fortunately blessed by the Sun and the Moon. Thus, the reins of command were not subverted. You tasked each [official] to adhere to their tasks and duties, preventing minor divisions from sowing. Using this as an inference, though intimate ministers and noble kin may possess influence and control troops, their status should be established beforehand. If the feudal lords command troops while being similar in strength, balanced in power, akin in favor, and equal in love, then they will not concede to each other. If they do not concede to each other, then their intentions will be different. Right now, the troops that the five colonel battalions lead are often no more than hundreds. In selectingColonels, do so according to their seniority and type for a proper match. As for heavy and major responsibilities, then for those who are capable of upholding them, you should, in your sagacious mindset, select one or two people who are like[Chen] Ping, [Zhou] Bo [周勃], Jin [Midi] [金日磾],Huo [Guang], and Liu Zhang [劉章], then gradually distinguish them in their power and importance so they can subdue and safeguard against each other. This will benefit many matters.” TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zisaid, “Right. It is as you said, I should plan for my prescient ruminations. As of today, who are those equal to[Chen] Pingand [Zhou] Bo [周勃], similar to Jin [Midi] [金日磾] andHuo [Guang], and can pair with Liu Zhang [劉章]?”[Sun] Zisaid, “I have heard that those who can discern men are intelligent; emperors find it hard to do such. In the sagacity ofTangandYu, for all of those whom they promoted and appointed, they would have observed and verified them according to their achievements. WhenChen Pingfirst served theProgenitor ofHan, the likes of [theMarquisof] Jiang [Zhou Bo [周勃]] and Guan [Ying] [灌嬰] defamed[Chen] Pingas having the crime of seizing gold by robbing his sister-in-law. Zhou Bo [周勃] played a flute and drew strong [bows], then began to serveSupremeProgenitor, and he also did not have a famous reputation then. TheSupremeProgenitorexamined their past actions, then afterwards, he discerned them and entrusted them with major affairs.Huo Guanghandled matters for over twenty years, being mindful, careful, and prudent, and was thus intimately trusted. Jin [Midi] [金日磾] was of uncivilized people, but as he was extremely filial and upright in demeanor, he was given special promotions and appointments, even as [imperial] attendants said, ‘You are crazy to have this son of barbarians and still regard him as important and valuable’. Although[Chen] Pingand [Zhou] Bo [周勃] safeguarded successions for theHan, in their ends, [Zhou] Bo [周勃] received a reputation of rebelling while[Chen] Pingfell into decline and removed himself due to being slandered by Lü Xu [呂須]. Shangguan Jie [上官桀] andSang Hongyangcontended for power withHuo Guangand almost resulted in disaster and chaos. Verily, it is not easy to discern a man, and it is challenging to serve as a minister. As for your selections, they should be whoYour Majestyis intimate with and whoYour Majestytrusts. This is truly not something that this foolish minister of yours is capable of distinguishing.”
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would comment on this.Sun [Zi]and[Liu] Fangwere called “Duty Monopolizers” in their time, controlling privy information. Among government affairs, there was not one they did not handle. When[Sun] Ziand[Liu] Fangwere asked questions regarding entrusting affairs, they should have made a decision between safety and danger. Instead, they were evasive in their replies without [distinguishing] right and wrong. When one is entrusted with intimate responsibilities by others, how could that be logical? According to the base biographies and other books, they all say that[Liu] Fangand[Sun] ZicommendedCao Shuangand urged the summoning of theCommanding King. The destruction of theWei domainhad its ruin rooted in this. TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zicame from his own family. Its intent is to conceal his major faults, fearful he had the blemish of failing a nation. But ultimately, none of them could be grounded away.
When thePrinceofQi[齊王] ascended the throne, as[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zidecided upon such a major plan, their fiefs increased by three hundred households. For[Liu] Fang, it merged with the previous [households] to make one thousand and one hundred [households], while[Sun] Zihad one thousand households. Their favored son was madeDistrict Marquis, their second son was madeCavalry Commandant, and the other sons were given the title ofPalace Gentleman.
In the first year of theera [240],[Liu] Fangwas given the title of LeftBrilliantly Blessed GrandeeandSun Ziwas given the title of RightBrilliantly Blessed Grandee. They received gold seals with purple ribbons and were given courtesy akin to theThree Excellencies.
In the sixth year [245],[Liu] Fangwas transferred toGeneral of Courserswhile[Sun] ZibecameGeneral of the Guards. They remained asSupervisorandDirector.
In the seventh year [246], a son was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquis. They each left their positions out of old age. AsRanged Marquises, they attended court on the first and fifteenth lunar days, and they were given the position ofSpecially Advanced.GAfterCao Shuangwas executed,[Sun] Ziwas madePalace AttendantandDirector of the Central Depositoryagain.
TheSeparate Biography of [Sun] Zistates: WhenGrand GeneralCao Shuangheld sole control over affairs, he altered many of the old regulations.[Sun] Zisighed, “For several generations, I have received favor, in addition to participating in entrusted responsibilities. Now that I cannot assist in current affairs, how can I sit and receive a salary by doing nothing?” Thus, he insisted that he was ill. In the second month of the ninth year [248], he was gifted an edict that stated:
“You, sir, have controlled privy information for over thirty years and managed general affairs. These merits were evident in previous administrations. When I succeeded the throne, I relied on your good ideas when I acted. This is why you were given high favor and praise in the past akin to theThree Excellencies. In exterior matters, you led various offices, while in interior matters, you offered forthright words. You have made the excuse that you are elderly in age and have a severe sickness, and so you have returned your seal and ribbon. You have been solemn throughout your history and your rhetoric has been most sincere. Heaven and Earth, through great obeisance, achieved virtue, while the noble man, through being upstanding and merciful, achieved benevolence. Heavy are your responsibilities, which are in violation of your wishes, sir. Now, we shall heed your request. You shall be gifted one million cash. TheMinister of the HouseholdandMinister Stewardshall be sent with a personal edict to remedy your illness in your mansion. Sir, you should strive to take medicine to strengthen your spirit and enhance your energy so as to prolong your boundless fortune. You shall be made Attendant who Courses. We additionally grant you a daily meal salary of meat and wine.”
During the second year of theera [250],[Liu] Fangdied. His posthumous title was theRespectedMarquis[敬侯]. His son,[Liu] Zheng, succeeded him.H[Sun] Ziagain gave up his position to return to his mansion. He was conferred to beGeneral of Coursersand transferred to becomePalace AttendantandSpecially Advancedlike before. In the third year [251], he died. His posthumous title was theChasteMarquis[貞侯]. His son,[Sun] Hong, succeeded him.
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would comment on this. TheBoss who Rebukes [Qin] Ziyustates that MinisterLiu Xu, styledWensheng, was the younger brother of[Liu] Zheng. He andZhang Hua, part of six men, were all commended for having writing ability that was so dazzling. Their thoughts were clear and organized. During the time of theTenderEmperorofJin,[Liu] Xuserved asColonel of Charging Cavalry.
[Liu] Fang, in talent and strategy, was superior to[Sun] Zi, but in cultivating moral behavior, could not compare.[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Ziwere good at receiving [orders] and obeying their sovereign, but there was never an instance they openly discussed gains and losses. They curbedXin Piand helpedWang Si, and it was for this they received derision from the world. Yet, in their time, whenever ministers made candid remonstrations, they supported their intentions. Also at that time, they would secretly explain advantages and disadvantages, they did not only focus on flattering.
During theera, the Five Ranks system was established. As[Liu] Fangand[Sun] Zihad manifest achievements in the previous administration,[Liu] Zhengchanged in fief to becomeViscountofFangcheng[方城子] while[Sun] HongbecameViscountofLishi[離石子].I
According to theGenealogy of the Sun Clan:[Sun] Hongserved asGrand AdministratorofNanyang. The son of[Sun] Hongwas [Sun] Chu [孫楚], who was styled Zijing [子荊].
TheChronicles of Jinstates: A fellow townsman of [Sun] Chu was [Wang] Ji [王濟], a noble son of a famous personage. He originally served asGrand Rectifierfor his home province. When a questioner came to ask how the character of [Sun] Chu could be described, [Wang] Ji said, “With regards to this man, you, sir, are incapable of assessing his reputation.” He then personally described him, saying, “His innate talent is outstanding and extensive, and his uprightness stands out as extraordinary.” [Sun] Chu would attain the position ofArmy Protector who Suppresses CaitiffsandGrand AdministratorofPingyi. The son of [Sun] Chu was [Sun] Xun [孫洵], who served asGrand AdministratorofYingchuan. The son of [Sun] Xun was[Sun] Sheng, who was styledAnguo. He served asPalace ServantandSupervisor of Confidential Depository. His younger cousin was [Sun] Chuo [孫綽], who was styled Xinggong [興公]. He served asAdministrator of Law Enforcement. [Sun] Chu,[Sun] Sheng, and [Sun] Chuo all had literary talent.[Sun] Shengwas especially good at talking about reputations and logic. All of his various works have been passed onto this world.

sections.liu-fang-ziqi:
劉放 (子棄)
Lived AD ca. 170–250
